sentences.text: Yeah, by the police.
On-- on-- on the--
Yeah, in the evening.
Yeah, we had to stay there, like I said.
And like I told you before, I woke up in the middle of the night when-- when Francesca came to say goodbye.
Then, I know early in the morning, my mother tried to get in touch by phone-- there was a phone in the school, and everyone was lined up to make calls-- to my uncles in Holland.
I had two-- two uncles from my father's side, the two brothers of my father who lived in Holland.
One was Max Spiegel and the other one was Adolf Spiegel.
And then I had an aunt, a sister of my mother, who had married a Dutch Jew and lived in a town called Enschede.
And apparently, she must have spoken to him sometime during the day or the next day, I don't quite remember the sequence, and made arrangements for us to go to Gennep, which was the-- Adolf.
It was-- Adolf was the oldest brother of my father and had also been my father's partner in-- in the business.
And I also remembered him well from-- still from-- from Germany.
The other uncle, I knew him by name, and I'd been to his place in Holland on visit, but I-- I don't remember him from Germany.
He had left much earlier.
So a day or two later-- I-- it could have been a week later, I don't know the exact time-- we were-- went by train to Holland.
You know, it's-- it's a little bit vague.
I-- I don't-- I-- as far as I remember, I went from the school to Holland.
But I know that my mother had been-- was allowed back to pick up things.
I don't think I went back, I don't-- because I don't remember being back in the house.
I'm sure I probably would have remembered it.
Because everything was smashed.
I don't think there would have been any point to it in any case at that time.
She-- this gentleman who lived upstairs, who was an ex-policeman, tried to protect her when she was around, you know, use his influence.
And we were taken to Holland by somebody, who I didn't know who it was, but I found out again years later this was a non-Jewish lady who was working as a-- as a maid for a-- for a Jewish family.
And she was Dutch, had a Dutch passport or was Dutch.
And she took us across the border.
Basically, illegally.
You know.
A lot of the German Jews tried to cross the border illegally.
And even though the border was closed officially, after the Kristallnacht, the Dutch sort of let people cross and then put them in camps, you know, of their own.
But as we had somewhere to go, we were-- we didn't have to go anywhere.
We went straight to-- to Gennep.
It was like going on a regular trip, basically.
There was no-- no incident, really.
No, no, no.
Just my sister and myself, OK, not my mother.
She had to-- she remained behind because she was trying to-- like wind up her affairs and-- and get a visa to somewhere, whatever.
Because I don't think she wanted to stay in Holland.
I don't think she could stay in Holland, really.
The Dutch didn't-- didn't want you either.
I mean, they'd let you cross the border, but they preferred you to continue from there.
Anyway, so--
No.
No, no.
No, no.
But when I got to Holland, I knew already that he'd been released.
OK.
And he arrived maybe-- anything between, I don't know.
A couple of weeks to a month later, he arrived also in Gennep, OK, that I remember.
He might-- I think it was a few weeks later.
No, no, train.
By train.
She took us by train.
No, she brought us across the border and Adolf met us I think at the first or second station in Holland.
Because he-- we-- he lived in a [INAUDIBLE] town called Gennep, which was fairly close to the border, even though it wasn't on the main rail line.
I think he picked up-- us up, if I remember, in a town called Nijmegen, which was-- could have been Nijmegen or Arnhem, one of the-- one-- because both are on the-- on the main railroad.
And-- and both of them are fairly close to Gennep.
Nijmegen is close.
Arnhem is a little bit further away, but also not very far.
And he picked us up.
And I think we went by bus from there to-- to Gennep.
I didn't mind so much.
My sister did.
You know, my sister was four years older, so she-- I guess emotionally for her, it was a little different.
You know, for me, it was-- it wasn't-- it wasn't fun.
You know.
It was strange, but I-- I knew-- I knew them well.
Adolf, I knew him well.
You know.
And-- and-- and there are two cousins.
Had two cousins there, two-- two girls, you know?
They both were older.
So I didn't mind going there.
And I felt-- I felt OK.
I'm sure I missed my mother, but I very quickly, you know, made friends, learned Dutch.
At that age, you learn very quickly by playing outside.
I started going to school.
It's a regular-- in that area, they had always two schools.
Most of Holland has two [INAUDIBLE].
There's a very big Catholic population.
They have a Catholic school and then a general school.
OK.
So everybody who wasn't Catholic went to the general school.
So all the Jewish kids of course, went to the general school.
And in that town-- in that little town, there were not-- were not that many Jews.
In Holland, they-- the Jews in almost every-- every town, every little place were Dutch Jews, not-- not German Jews.
And in this particular little town, there were just a few families.
But when the German Jews came, that's when the Jewish population relatively grew.
It still wasn't very big.
You know, it wasn't.
There was no-- there was no proper synagogue.
It was like a little room, you know, where they prayed.
And-- and I would say the majority were German Jews, yeah, not-- not Dutch Jews.
But life was, except for my mother not being there, was very normal, you know, because Holland wasn't at war or anything.
And neither was Germany [INAUDIBLE] still.
But, you know, there was no anti-Semitic laws.
I mean, the Jews were treated like everybody else.
The only thing is we were-- we were foreigners, basically.
We weren't Dutch.
OK.
But even that didn't really bother us, except there was sort of an incident which I remember well because it sort of was, you know, a big thing.
I had two uncles who came to live at the same house.
These were the husbands of two sisters of my father.
My father had a lot of brothers and sisters.
There were seven all together.

So two of his sisters, their husbands, who were cousins coincidentally-- with the name of Hes-- they came to-- after the Kristallnacht, after they were released from prison, they came to stay in Holland while their wives made arrangements to get a visa to go to Chile.
They had a-- they could get-- they could go to Chile.
They got visa to go to Chile, and they found this through my mother.
My mother had the opportunity to go to Chile, but she felt that Chile was the end of the world.
Didn't want to go to Chile.
She was looking for North America, or whatever.
She wasn't looking for Chile.
She didn't-- she thought it was too far away.
So she told them about it.
And they made arrangements and-- and got the visa to go to Chile.
In the meanwhile, the two men were staying with us.
Yeah, from Germany.
From a different town.
OK.
A town called [INAUDIBLE].
It was a small town, in those days especially.
So what happening?
Obviously, they must have crossed the border illegally.
And also, my uncle must have done something to help them which wasn't-- the Germans didn't like.
I don't know exactly what he did.
I guess it must have been monetary again.
You know, even though they didn't have much money-- they weren't very wealthy people.
But he must have done something because the Dutch came to arrest them and-- and my uncle.
Yeah.
But they did it under the pressure from the Germans.
OK.
And they came and picked him up, which I remember because it was a shock, you know, my uncle being arrested.
And both-- all uncles.
I mean, you look-- look upon uncles as, you know, something special.
And-- and were, actually.
[INAUDIBLE] All of them-- I was spoiled by them, actually, being the little boy.
And they were sent to a prison further south called [INAUDIBLE] where they were kept.
And then, those two uncles, I didn't see them again then because they were-- when they were released, they went via Italy and to-- to South America, to Chile.
And my uncle was released fairly soon afterwards at night.
The Dutch didn't really want to imprison him apparently, but imprisoned him under the pressure of the Germans.
And there were some Nazis in town.
You know, Holland had a fairly large, relatively speaking, Nazi party.
Active.
OK.
They called them something else.
They don't call themselves Nazis, but it was the same thing.
You know.
And they make-- they, I guess, put the pressure onto the police.
So they released him at night.
And he came home at night when nobody saw him, but he came back maybe a week later.
I-- I think it was in 1939.
Must have been 1939.
But before the war actually started.
I-- I think it was in the summer of '39.
It was a few months before the war started.
It was before my mother went to England that I know.
So it could have been any time-- June, July of 1939, around that time.
My aunt, of course.
Marthe.
And then, two cousins, [?
Margaret ?]-- or Margot, as you say, and Alice.
Alice was two years older than I am, and Margot was six years older, five or six years older than I am.
And anyway, so my uncle came back.
And nothing much happened after that, you know, as far as that's concerned.
I don't think he went out very much.
You know.
I think he was told just to-- to lay low.
You know, you can stay home, but just to lay low.
And then, the next thing I remember is when my mother came to visit.
We have heard she is going to come and see us for a week.
She's gonna stay with us for a week, and then continue to England.
Yeah, she had been in Dinslaken.
Tried to sell the house, which was very difficult, of course.
She sold it in the end for nothing.
I mean, there's always somebody willing to buy for a few-- relatively pennies and-- and-- and make it look legal.
And then to, you know, sort of pack the few things she had.
She could take some things with her-- clothes, things like that.
And she got this visa to go to England.
She couldn't get anything else.
She did-- she had given up on Chile.
And there was nothing else available.
You know, you couldn't go to-- I don't think she really wanted to go to Palestine.
If she wanted to, the-- the-- it was closed.
The British had closed Palestine.
And this country was closed.
And-- and everything was closed, basically.
And I think she would have wanted to go to Argentina even, but she couldn't get that either.
Then, she got this au pair-- like au pair-- is it called au pair-- visa to England through a cousin of hers who was living there.
And they specified-- the visa specified that she could only come as a maid, nothing else.
And she couldn't bring anything with her, any-- anybody with her.
So she decided to take this.
She got this in August of '39.
She decided to take this because this was a way out.
And she figured that she would go to England and we would come whenever things were-- whenever she would settle down and she could bring us over.
Holland was neutral after all.
And if there's going to be a war, nothing would happen to us in Holland.
This was her consideration.
On September 1, she came to visit us.
And instead of staying a week, she-- she-- the same day she went she left because my uncle said to her that he just heard on the news that the Germans had invaded Poland.
And he said, there's gonna be a war.
And they're going to stop passenger ships, so you better leave.
So she managed to get the last ship, passenger ship, from Holland to England.
[INAUDIBLE] Holland is sort of a little-- little harbor, which from there you take ships to England.
In those days, you didn't fly.
You took a ship.
And this-- I didn't know at the time that it was the last ship, that I found out after the war.
But even then, I was disappointed that she only stayed for one day.
But it-- you know, it didn't bother me that much.
I thought, OK, another few months, or whatever.
You know, in the meanwhile, I'm having a good time here.
I'm going to school.
I make friends.
Not so-- not so terrible.
Life wasn't-- wasn't that terrible.
And the war did start, but it didn't affect us very much, at least us kids didn't affect us very much.
Because like I said, Holland at the beginning was neutral.
And we continued to go to school.
And the Nazis were active, but they were the minority.
They were-- they-- they were vocal, but they were the minority.
They were not in the government.
And if there was any anti-Semitism, I didn't notice it.
Except for them, you know, and they were obvious.
They-- This went on till May 1940.
OK.
Now, I remember the date.
That's one of the few dates I remember exactly because it sort of-- it was a milestone.
It was May 10, 1940.
May's a beautiful month in Holland.
In Holland, you have a lot of rain.
But May usually is no rain and a lot of sun.
It was a sunny day, May 10, 1940.
And I woke up, and it was very noisy.
I could hear like a lot of planes in the sky, a lot of noise.
We lived by a main road.
A lot of noise on the road.
In those days, there weren't many cars.
And all of a sudden, it sounded like a whole army was down there.
And it was.
When I got up and looked out the window, I saw the German army.
And tanks, and-- and trucks, and whatever.
And opposite us was a large, sort of empty place.
Empty-- big, empty place which was used for fairs.
You know, for-- once a year, there was-- in-- in German Dutch, they call it [INAUDIBLE].
It's like a fair, you know, with-- with all sort of things.
And it was empty, so there were trucks and tanks parked there already.
And a lot of planes in the sky.
These were German planes.
And it was-- so like, what's going on?
You know, and the Germans had invaded Holland.
As a matter of fact, when I came downstairs, my uncle was gone because he knew that they would be looking for him very soon.
When my aunt was making coffee, a German soldier knocked on the door and very civilly, you know, very nicely had asked-- he brought some coffee with him.
Could she make some coffee for him, and-- and-- which she said, why not?
And then he asked me to look at-- stand at the window to watch whether his unit was leaving, I should tell him, you know, so that he wouldn't miss his-- his truck or whatever he was with.
And he sat there, drunk his coffee, didn't say very much.
And-- and-- said, thank you very much and walked out.
And the next few days, like we-- like all other kids, we went to the camp.
You know, they had sort of like an encampment, you know, where they-- where they had the army.
And we-- to-- to see them and-- and-- and their-- sort of-- it was-- it was a fascinating thing in a way for-- for kids.
And I do remember-- what I do remember was some Dutch soldiers who had shed their uniform after, you know, when they saw the invasion, and were-- Why do I remember it?
Because they all were wearing clothes which didn't fit them.
You know, that's how you could know it was a soldier.
Because some-- some civilian had given them clothes and they usually didn't fit.
So we knew these were soldiers who had been surprised by the invasion and not wanting to be taken prisoner, so they were wearing, you know, ill-fitting civilian clothes.
But we didn't care.
You know, we went to the German camp, and they gave us candy even.
You know, they tried to be nice at the beginning.
Especially in-- in-- in Western Europe, especially in Holland, they tried to be civilized.
You know, they-- they considered the Dutch to be kinsmen.
You know.

And there even was an occasion where-- and this is sort of-- I always-- always remember this where-- I don't know whether it was-- I don't think it was-- I think it was just a regular soldier who was giving us candy or something.
And one of the boys said to me, you don't want to give it to him.
He's a Jew.
You know, so the soldier turned around to him and said, I didn't ask you.
I didn't ask your opinion, you know, which was sort of-- was very strange.
You know, but at the time, sort of, I took it in stride.
You know, I thought it was very nice of him.
But this was just the first week or two, you know, when they were that civilized.
You know, and then-- first of all, after a few days, the Gestapo-- Gestapo is the German secret police.
You know, [INAUDIBLE].
They knocked on the door to look for my uncle.
OK.
And of course, he was gone.
Like he knew-- he knew that they would look for him.
He had taken his bicycle and had-- took the-- and was going into Holland, deeper into Holland.
You know, he-- he thought maybe he could out-cycle the German army.
You know, like-- like everybody, nobody realized that, you know, that Holland would very quickly capitulate.
You know.
And he was cycling in the direction of the-- the interior provinces.
Or I would say Holland-- Holland itself.
You know, like the-- like Amsterdam or this area.
And supposedly-- and this he told me again many years later.
Supposedly, the Dutch, their defense was the dikes.
They would make holes in the dikes and Holland would be underwater.
And he said, they did.
But he said he was cycling through it.
It was so shallow that he could cycle though.
It didn't mean anything, you know, the-- the water at that time.
Yeah.
Yes.
And they looked all over, didn't find my uncle.
And of course, my aunt didn't know where he was, you know, which was smart.
And then they left.
You know.
And after, then they came back a few days later and took my aunt, OK, to question her.
They took her to a town in Germany called Kleve.
That's where they-- I think they had their headquarters.
So when that happened, we had to leave to go to a different uncle, this other brother who lived in a village called Dinxperlo.
So now you are your sister and your cousins?
And-- and--
No, no, just my sister and myself.
The cousins remained behind.
OK.
My aunt said before she left, you know, that-- that we should leave.
OK.
My aunt said when she was taken away that it would be better because we were younger, you know, that we should leave.
It'd be safer for us to go to this other uncle.
And he wanted us to come there, this other uncle.
And-- and-- so we went there.
By bus.
Partly by ourselves.
He picked us up.
We had to change bus in-- in a town called [INAUDIBLE].
And that's where he was waiting for us.
I'm just trying to think.
This was in 1940.
So I was eight, so she was 12.
And-- and the cousins, one was 14, another one was 16.
So it's a difference, you know, that age.
And the 16-year-old one, Margot, she was a very-- very adult.
You know, very-- well, but they both were, as a matter of fact.
Anyway, I tell you more about them in a little while because one of them, unfortunately, died.
I tell you that when I get to it.
Uncle Max.
Originally, actually was Moses, but he called himself Max.
Yeah.
Yeah.
He's a younger brother of Adolf and my-- he was younger than my father, too.
OK.
And-- and we went to Dinxperlo.
We also had two cousins, one boy and one girl.
The girl was much older.
She-- at the time, already was, I would say, around 17, 18.
Name was Ruth.
And Alfred, my cousin, was a year older than I am.
Wild, very wild.
And Dinxperlo was a real village.
You know, partially agricultural.
And the houses were-- there was a lot of room around each house.
There were fields with corn around the house.
We still could go to school.
I went to the general school again with Alfred.
And I remember my aunt writing to my mother that I imitate everything Alfred is doing.
You know.
[LAUGHS] And we stayed there only for a short while because we went back to Gennep after my aunt was released.
They released my aunt, strangely enough.
OK.
I guess they-- they-- the reason-- I don't know what the reason, I can't read their mind.
But there probably were two reasons.
First of all, she didn't know anything.
Secondly, maybe they thought by releasing her, they could trace my uncle.
You know, whatever the reason, but they released her.
And so then she wanted us back there.
So we went back for a short while.
Because then my younger cousin, Alice, got very sick.
And she had to go into the hospital.
And she had to take care of her.
So that's when we left the second time.
We left and go to-- to go to Dinxperlo.
It was just a very short interlude.
Maybe a few months, I don't know exactly the-- the time span.
And this cousin died, unfortunately.
She had a-- like a tumor on the brain I think it was.
It was a form of cancer, I guess.
And she died in the end of 1940, I think it was already.
She died.
And then this-- the aunt after that went-- went into hiding together with the other-- my other cousin.
You know, they never-- she didn't return to the house.
She lived in that house while-- while my cousin was in the-- in the hospital.
But after that, they went into hiding.
So that's why we never went back to them.
Yeah.
They didn't-- they didn't hide in Gennep.
They hid somewhere else.
I don't know exactly where because they--
No.
No, no.
Apparently, he wanted to see his sick child.
So the-- the hospital, some of the nurses who were-- who were trustworthy-- not everybody could you could trust-- arranged for him to visit her at night to see her.
So he did visit her several times in the middle of the night.
She was in the private room.
And they saw her.
And they also saw her before she died.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah, yeah.
Right.
This is 1941 already.
And this is where you see those Red Cross letters where my mother asked, how come you-- what are you-- how come you're now in Dinxperlo?
And then when my aunt wrote back to them that we are now staying there.
Didn't give the reason.
You know.
There was no-- you couldn't-- you could only write 25-- we couldn't write letters, normal letters.
You could write letters to the Red Cross.
Every-- every month or two months, you could write if you wanted to.
You could write 25 words.
So basically, you could say, well-- and you couldn't write anything you wanted to, obviously.
You know.
And-- and the same letter, oh, my mother would write on the back or she would write another letter, and we would write it on the back.
You know, that's-- that's how it worked.
Was this because of the war or was it particularly because of Jews?
Yeah.
No, no, it was the war.
There was no-- and England was the enemy as far as Germany was concerned.
And-- and-- for instance, somewhere I have a letter from an uncle in the United States who wrote to us still in 1941.
Because United States was not at war with-- with Germany yet.
And as a matter of fact, he sent a letter from-- my mother had sent him a letter, which he then sent to us.
OK.
That was in 1941.
A regular letter, which he forwarded and he wrote himself also.
But then once, you know, once you are at war, the only way-- it went like this.
It went from the-- from the Dutch Red Cross to the German Red Cross.
And the Germany Red Cross to the Swiss Red Cross.
And then the Swiss Red Cross to the British Red Cross.
And that's how it worked.
And in reverse.
And we received those letters once in a while.
And this was a sign to my mother that we were still alive and-- and everything was fine.
She asked once, why didn't you-- why didn't you send anything from Gennep?
So that's when my-- my uncle wrote back-- I think I showed it to you, one of the letters.
He said, Gennep and-- and Dinxperlo, everything is fine.
The children are on vacation in Enschede.
And in two lines, he sort of covered the whole family.
And we did actually go to Enschede.
Enschede was the town where the-- my aunt lived.
You know, the sister of my mother with-- with her husband.
And she had a little baby at the time.
His name was Abraham.
He was at the time two years old or three years old.
Yeah.
Yeah, three years old.
And we used to visit them in summer.
Summer '41, Summer '42, even. '42-- [INAUDIBLE] yes, I think so.
Because a little later, they were picked up in '42.
It must have been in the early summer.
It was quite a trip.
We went by bus, but there was no gasoline.
So the buses were-- went on-- they had in a back like a huge boiler.
And it was a wood-burning boiler.
And some of these functions.
I have never seen ever since anywhere in the world.
It was very ingenious.
You know, they broke down a lot on the way, but they used to sort of put a big-- it was a big, huge boiler on the back.
A round thing, which was put onto the back.
And this somehow got the bus moved.
The gas from the-- from wood and coal in, but mostly wood got the bus moving somehow.
I guess they must have made some adjustments to the engines, but these were very-- very ingenious way of getting-- getting around.
And-- and this-- this was a mode of transportation.
Everything was rationed already in-- in '41 and '42.
But as we're living in a village, it was-- an agricultural area, it wasn't that bad.
You know, I mean, we weren't hungry.
There were no certain luxuries.
I mean, things like oranges and bananas obviously didn't exist, because they came from other areas.
But the basics, like-- like bread and-- and potatoes and even vegetables, were available.
You know, and-- so-- and life was fairly normal because the Germans, even though they were in the area, there was not-- there was no big occupying force in that village, or Dinxperlo.
The first thing which I remember, what sort of made things change was when we-- when the law came-- we couldn't go to the school anymore.
